name: The Fates
description: The Fates, also known as the Moirai, are the goddesses of destiny in Greek mythology. They are typically depicted as three sisters, each responsible for a different aspect of human life. Clotho spins the thread of life, Lachesis measures its length, and Atropos cuts it, determining the moment of death. Even the gods were said to be subject to their decisions, highlighting their immense power and influence. Their parentage varies depending on the source, sometimes described as daughters of Nyx (Night), other times as daughters of Zeus and Themis, or even as primordial beings existing from the very beginning of time. Regardless of their origin, their role is consistent: to control the thread of life for every mortal and immortal being. They are often portrayed as stern and unyielding, representing the inevitability of fate and the limitations of free will. While mortals might try to alter their destiny, the Fates ultimately hold the final sway. They are not necessarily malevolent, but rather impartial arbiters of destiny, ensuring that the cosmic order is maintained. Their decisions are often perceived as mysterious and inscrutable, reflecting the unpredictable nature of life itself. Shrines and offerings were made to the Fates in hopes of securing a favorable destiny, though their decrees were considered absolute and unchangeable.
subtitle: Spinners, Measurers, and Cutters of Fate

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Meleager
keyMyths.description: The Fates appear at Meleager's birth and foretell that he will live only as long as a specific log remains unburnt. His mother hides the log, but later destroys it in a fit of rage, leading to his death.

keyMyths.title: Admetus and Apollo
keyMyths.description: Apollo tricks the Fates into allowing Admetus to live beyond his appointed time, provided someone else takes his place. His wife, Alcestis, volunteers, showcasing a rare instance of defying fate.

keyMyths.title: The Fates and Zeus
keyMyths.description: Even Zeus, the king of the gods, is subject to the decrees of the Fates. He cannot overturn their decisions, demonstrating their ultimate authority over all beings.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: References to the Fates and their power over even the gods.
